class_schedule.topic: Global Health Governance: Architecture and Challenges
learning_objectives: Map the global health governance architecture: WHO, GAVI, Global Fund, World Bank
Analyze the tension between sovereignty and collective health security
Evaluate the International Health Regulations as a governance framework

lecture_notes.introduction: Global health is where biology meets politics. Diseases do not respect borders, but the institutions charged with addressing them are constrained by sovereignty.
key_concepts: Global health governance: WHO at the center, surrounded by a complex web
International Health Regulations: obligations for disease surveillance and response
GAVI, Global Fund, and the vertical vs. horizontal health debate
Health as a foreign policy issue: security, development, and human rights framings
Pharmaceutical governance: patents, access, and the TRIPS Agreement
lecture_notes.demonstration: Institutional mapping: who does what in the global health architecture

review.value: The legally binding framework requiring WHO member states to detect, report, and respond to public health emergencies of international concern, revised after SARS in 2005

The tension between WTO intellectual property rules that protect pharmaceutical patents and developing countries' need for affordable medicines, partially addressed by the Doha Declaration on TRIPS and Public Health

class_schedule.topic: Pandemics: Lessons from COVID-19 and Beyond
learning_objectives: Analyze the global response to COVID-19: successes, failures, and inequities
Evaluate pandemic preparedness and the push for a pandemic treaty
Assess vaccine equity and the politics of global health emergencies
readings: Independent Panel for Pandemic Preparedness, COVID-19: Make It the Last Pandemic (excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: COVID-19 exposed every weakness in global health governance. Understanding what went wrong -- and what went right -- is essential for preparing for the next pandemic.
key_concepts: COVID-19 timeline: emergence, response, vaccination, aftermath
Early warning failures: IHR notification delays, WHO limitations, intelligence failures
Vaccine nationalism vs. COVAX: equity in pandemic response
Supply chain vulnerabilities: PPE, tests, therapeutics, vaccines
Pandemic treaty negotiations: what a new agreement might include
lecture_notes.demonstration: After-action review: evaluating one country's COVID-19 response using pandemic preparedness criteria
setup_requirements: COVID-19 response timeline for selected country

review.value: The tendency of wealthy countries to hoard vaccine supplies for their own populations before sharing with poorer countries, undermining the global response and prolonging the pandemic

The global initiative co-led by WHO, GAVI, and CEPI to ensure equitable access to COVID-19 vaccines, which delivered over 1.9 billion doses but was undermined by supply hoarding and export restrictions

class_schedule.topic: HIV/AIDS, Tuberculosis, and Malaria: The Big Three
learning_objectives: Analyze the global burden of HIV/AIDS, TB, and malaria
Evaluate the effectiveness of global health interventions: PEPFAR, Global Fund
Assess the political economy of infectious disease control

lecture_notes.introduction: The fight against HIV/AIDS, TB, and malaria has been the defining global health campaign of the past three decades. Its successes and ongoing challenges hold lessons for all global health efforts.
key_concepts: HIV/AIDS: from death sentence to chronic disease (for some)
PEPFAR: the largest bilateral health program in history
Global Fund model: pooled financing, country ownership, performance-based funding
TB: the forgotten pandemic killing 1.3 million annually
Malaria: progress through bed nets, ACTs, and vaccines -- but resistance threatens gains
lecture_notes.demonstration: Data analysis: tracking HIV/AIDS treatment coverage and mortality across regions

review.value: The US President's Emergency Plan for AIDS Relief, launched in 2003, has invested over $100 billion and saved an estimated 25 million lives through antiretroviral treatment and prevention programs
A financing mechanism that pools contributions from governments and the private sector, channels them through country-led programs, and ties continued funding to measurable results

class_schedule.topic: Non-Communicable Diseases and Social Determinants of Health
learning_objectives: Analyze the global burden of NCDs: heart disease, cancer, diabetes, mental health
Evaluate the social determinants of health framework
Assess the challenges of governing NCDs compared to infectious diseases
readings: WHO, Global Action Plan for the Prevention and Control of NCDs (overview)

lecture_notes.introduction: Non-communicable diseases now kill more people globally than infectious diseases, but the global health governance architecture was built for infectious disease. NCDs require a different approach.
key_concepts: NCD burden: 74% of global deaths from heart disease, cancer, diabetes, respiratory disease
Social determinants of health: the conditions in which people are born, grow, live, and work
Health inequity: preventable differences in health outcomes linked to poverty, gender, race
Corporate vectors: tobacco, alcohol, ultra-processed food industries
WHO FCTC as a model for NCD governance
lecture_notes.demonstration: Social determinants mapping: tracing how income, education, and environment shape health outcomes in two countries

review.value: The non-medical factors that shape health outcomes: income, education, employment, housing, food systems, social support networks, and structural discrimination

Industries (tobacco, alcohol, ultra-processed food) whose products are primary drivers of NCDs, exercising political influence to resist regulation -- a governance challenge requiring strategies beyond traditional public health

class_schedule.topic: Forced Displacement, Humanitarian Crises, and Health
learning_objectives: Analyze the health challenges facing refugees and internally displaced populations
Evaluate humanitarian health response systems: UN agencies, MSF, ICRC
Assess the nexus between conflict, displacement, and public health

lecture_notes.introduction: Over 100 million people are forcibly displaced worldwide. Their health needs are immense, and the humanitarian system is stretched beyond capacity.
key_concepts: Forced displacement: refugees, IDPs, stateless persons -- numbers and trends
Health in humanitarian crises: communicable disease, malnutrition, mental health, SGBV
Humanitarian health actors: UNHCR, WFP, UNICEF, MSF, ICRC
Humanitarian principles: humanity, neutrality, impartiality, independence
Protracted displacement: when emergencies become permanent
lecture_notes.demonstration: Humanitarian response planning: designing a health response for a sudden-onset refugee crisis

review.value: The four principles guiding humanitarian action: humanity (alleviate suffering), neutrality (not taking sides), impartiality (based on need alone), independence (autonomous from political objectives)
The minimum standards for humanitarian response covering water supply, sanitation, food security, shelter, and health services -- the benchmarks for adequate humanitarian assistance

class_schedule.topic: Universal Health Coverage and the Future of Global Health
learning_objectives: Analyze universal health coverage as a development and human rights goal
Evaluate different models for achieving UHC in low and middle-income countries
Assess the future of global health governance
readings: WHO, Universal Health Coverage: Moving Towards Better Health (overview)
Kickbusch, 'Governing the Global Health Security Threat'

lecture_notes.introduction: Universal health coverage -- ensuring all people can access quality health services without financial hardship -- is the organizing goal for global health in the 21st century.
key_concepts: UHC definition: coverage, quality, and financial protection
Models: Beveridge (UK/NHS), Bismarck (Germany), national health insurance (Taiwan, Thailand)
UHC in LMICs: Rwanda, Thailand, and Mexico as models
Health financing: domestic resources, innovative financing, external aid
Global health governance reform: pandemic preparedness, One Health, antimicrobial resistance
lecture_notes.demonstration: Health system design exercise: designing a UHC path for a low-income country

review.value: The goal that all people receive the health services they need -- prevention, treatment, rehabilitation, palliative care -- without suffering financial hardship, enshrined in SDG Target 3.8
The approach recognizing that human, animal, and environmental health are interconnected, requiring cross-sector collaboration to address zoonotic diseases, antimicrobial resistance, and food safety

questions.question: What were the two most significant failures of the global health system in responding to COVID-19, and what reforms could address them?
questions.answer: First failure: early warning and information sharing. Despite the IHR requirement for timely notification, there were critical delays in China's reporting and WHO's declaration of a PHEIC. The IHR lacks enforcement mechanisms and depends on state cooperation. Reform: strengthen WHO's independent investigation authority, create an independent surveillance mechanism, and establish clearer triggers for emergency declarations that do not depend solely on affected state consent. Second failure: vaccine equity. Wealthy countries secured most early vaccine supplies through advance purchase agreements, leaving most of the Global South unvaccinated well into 2022. COVAX was underfunded and outcompeted. Reform: establish binding mechanisms for equitable allocation during emergencies, create regional manufacturing capacity (mRNA hubs in Africa), and negotiate pathogen access and benefit-sharing agreements that ensure equitable access.

rubric.key: Explains each failure clearly with specifics

questions.question: Explain the tension between the TRIPS Agreement and access to medicines in developing countries. How did the Doha Declaration attempt to resolve this tension?
questions.answer: TRIPS (Trade-Related Aspects of Intellectual Property Rights) requires WTO members to provide 20-year patent protection for pharmaceutical products, which enables companies to charge high prices to recoup R&D investment. This creates a tension with public health because many developing countries cannot afford patented medicines for HIV/AIDS, TB, malaria, and other diseases. The 2001 Doha Declaration on TRIPS and Public Health affirmed that TRIPS should be interpreted to support public health, explicitly recognizing countries' right to issue compulsory licenses (allowing generic production without patent holder consent during health emergencies) and to engage in parallel importation (importing cheaper versions from other countries). While the Doha Declaration was an important political statement, implementation remains contentious: pharmaceutical companies and some developed countries resist compulsory licensing, and many developing countries lack manufacturing capacity to produce generics.

questions.question: What are the social determinants of health and why do they explain more about health outcomes than healthcare systems alone?
questions.answer: Social determinants of health are the non-medical conditions that shape health outcomes: income and wealth, education, employment and working conditions, housing and neighborhood quality, food systems, social support networks, and exposure to discrimination and violence. They explain more than healthcare because the vast majority of health outcomes are determined before a person enters the healthcare system: poor nutrition, unsafe water, air pollution, hazardous work, stress from poverty, and lack of education all cause disease. Healthcare treats illness after it occurs, but social determinants create or prevent it. For example, life expectancy can differ by 20 years between the richest and poorest neighborhoods in the same city, even with the same healthcare system. This is why investing solely in hospitals and doctors without addressing poverty, education, and environment will not achieve health equity.

rubric.key: Explains why they matter more than healthcare alone

questions.question: What are the four humanitarian principles and why are they important for health organizations operating in conflict zones?
questions.answer: The four humanitarian principles are: (1) Humanity -- the imperative to prevent and alleviate suffering; (2) Neutrality -- not taking sides in hostilities or political controversies; (3) Impartiality -- providing assistance based solely on need, without discrimination; (4) Independence -- maintaining autonomy from political, military, and economic objectives of any party. They are critical for health organizations in conflict zones because: neutrality and impartiality allow organizations like MSF and ICRC to negotiate access to all parties in a conflict, treating wounded combatants and civilians on all sides; independence ensures that humanitarian health aid is not perceived as a tool of one party, which would endanger both health workers and patients; and adherence to these principles protects medical facilities from targeting under international humanitarian law. When principles are violated (e.g., when military forces use humanitarian operations for intelligence), health workers and patients face increased danger.

rubric.key: Explains their importance in conflict zones

major_assignments.title: Global Health Policy Analysis
major_assignments.description: Select a global health challenge and analyze the governance framework addressing it. Evaluate effectiveness and produce policy recommendations for strengthening the response.
objectives: Analyze global health governance for a specific challenge
Develop policy recommendations grounded in evidence and political feasibility

criteria: Thorough understanding of the health challenge
Multiple dimensions of the problem addressed
Comprehensive analysis of a global health challenge

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on global health governance, diseases, and social determinants

participation.description: Engagement in case discussions, data exercises, and simulations

major_assignments.description: Global health policy analysis paper and presentation

midterm_examination.description: Exam on health governance, pandemic response, and infectious diseases

final_examination.description: Comprehensive exam integrating all global health topics

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance expected. More than 3 unexcused absences reduce participation grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late papers penalized 5% per day. Policy briefs cannot be submitted late.
course_policies.collaboration: Data analysis collaboration encouraged. Written work individually authored.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work original. Misrepresenting health data results in course failure.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to WHO GHO, IHME GBD, and global health databases.
